---
title: "Observable JS — interactivity with no server"
description: "A slider the reader can drag, recomputing a curve in their own browser. Zero backend, zero install."
image: thumbnail.svg
image-alt: "Observable JS — interactivity with no server — Blood Doctor · Quarto"
date: "2026-07-18"
categories: [Engines, Interactive]
engine: markdown
---

R, Python and Julia run when the document is **built**. **Observable JS** (`ojs`)
runs when the document is **read** — in the reader's browser. That means genuine
interactivity on a plain static website, with no server to host or maintain.

## A reactive cell

````markdown
```{ojs}
viewof half_life = Inputs.range([2, 48], {value: 12, label: "t½ (hours)"})
```
```{ojs}
Plot.plot({ marks: [Plot.lineY(
  d3.range(0, 48, 0.5).map(t => ({t, C: 20*Math.exp(-Math.log(2)/half_life*t)})),
  {x: "t", y: "C", stroke: "#B71C1C"})] })
```
````

Drag the slider and every cell that depends on it recomputes instantly — the
reactive model made famous by [Observable](https://observablehq.com), built into
Quarto and powered by `d3` and `Observable Plot`.

## Why teachers love it

A trainee can *feel* how halving a drug's half-life changes its concentration
curve, or how a diagnostic threshold shifts sensitivity and specificity — by
moving a control, not by reading a caption.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Try it live
The [**Observable page**](/engines/observable.qmd) and the
[**flagship essay**](/posts/2026-07-22-knowledge-has-no-limits/) both carry a
working half-life slider you can drag right now.
:::
